[picks up cup, places cup on saucer] Newton came up with one more law when he started thinking about the interaction of objects. - He had already talked about what happens when there is no force (1st Law). - He then talked about what happens when there is a force (2nd Law). - But what happens when you have objects interacting, affecting each other? The 3rd Law (The Law of Action-Reaction) "For every action force there is an equal and opposite reaction force." Anytime an object applies a force to another object, there is an equal and opposite force back on the original object. - If you push on a wall you feel a force against your hand the wall is pushing back on you with as much force as you apply to it. - If this wasn't happening, your hand would accelerate through the wall! Standing on the ground, you push on the ground with a force due to gravity (Fg down) and the ground pushes back on you (FN up). - FN is the normal force. - It balances out the force due to gravity down. - The normal force is always perpendicular to the surface the object is on. - Without it there would be a net force down on you and you would accelerate down. - This is an example of an action-reaction pair, two forces that are equal but opposite to each other. There is one ultra important thing to remember when you are looking at action-reaction pairs. - The two forces that you are looking at are each acting on different objects! - If you are examining what you think are action-reaction forces, and they are both acting on the same object, they are not. - In the example above, Fg is the person pushing down on the ground, while FN is the ground pushing up on the person. Here are some examples of action-reaction forces that depend on the objects being in direct contact, meaning that the two objects involved are actually touching each other to exert forces on each other. These are called "contact forces." - Action: the tires on a car push on the road Reaction: the road pushes on the tires. - Action: while swimming, you push the water backwards... Reaction: the water pushes you forward. Action-reaction pairs can also happen without friction, or even with the objects not touching each other, known as "action at a distance" forces - Action: a rocket pushes out exhaust Reaction: the exhaust pushes the rocket forward. One of the original arguments that flight in the vacuum of space was impossible was that there would be nothing to push against. This action-reaction explains how a rocket can fly in space where there is no air to push against. - Action: the earth pulls down on a ball Reaction: ball pulls up on the earth. How can this second example be true?!? - There is an action-reaction pair of forces given by EFb = bFE - We know that the ball will accelerate towards the earth at 9.81 m/s2, but does the earth accelerate towards the ball at the same rate? - If this is true you would expect the earth to be constantly bouncing up towards falling objects. - Carefully remember Newton's 2nd Law (F = ma). - In this example the forces are equal, but the mass of the earth is considerably more than the ball! - The earth has more inertia than the ball. - Let's assume the ball has a mass of 2.00 kg and do some calculations The Force of the Earth on the Ball EFb = ma = mg = (2.00kg) (9.81m/s2) EFb = 19.6N This is the force of the Earth acting on the ball, but because of Newton's 3rd Law, it is also the force of the ball on the Earth. EFb = bFE = 19.6N The Acceleration of the Earth because of the Ball bFE = ma a = bFE / m = 19.6N / 5.98e24 kg a = 3.28e-24 m/s2 This is such a small acceleration of the Earth towards the ball that it can't even be measured. We can see that although the forces are equal, the accelerations do not have to be! Sir Isaac Newton hated his own theories about gravity being an "action at a distance" force. He believed so strongly that there must be some material that connects objects that have a gravitational pull on each other, that he was one of the first scientists to seriously suggest there was a mysterious substance called the aether (sometimes spelled ether) that connected all objects in the universe. Example 1: When a rifle fires a bullet, the force the rifle exerts on the bullet is exactly the same (but in the opposite direction) as the force the bullet exerts on the rifle so the rifle "kicks back". The bullet has a mass of 15 g and the rifle is 6.0 kg. The bullet leaves the 75 cm long rifle barrel moving at 70 m/s. a) Determine the acceleration of the bullet. Calculate this using a kinematics formula like vf2 = vi2 + 2ad and you should get 3.3e3 m/s2. b) Determine the force on the bullet. F = ma will let you calculate the answer 49 N. c) Determine the acceleration of the rifle. Again, use F = ma, but make sure that you use the correct mass. You should get 8.2 m/s2. d) Explain why the bullet accelerates more than the rifle if the forces are the same. Although have the same amount of force acting on them, they each have a different mass (and therefore a different inertia). Example 2: If I push on a lawn mower, it pushes back on me with an equal, but opposite force. Explain why we don't both just stay still. - The answer is that these forces are acting on different bodies (and there are other forces to consider). - It doesn't matter to the lawn mower that there is a force on me all that matters to the lawn mower is that there is a force on it, so it starts to move! - Another action-reaction pair you need to consider is that I am pushing backwards on the ground, and it pushes forwards on me. The tall ladder down there can get up to the top ones too. So did you have indoor or outdoor recess today? I read it to you on the airplane though remember? Title: Understanding Prime Numbers with Special Rings! Hello young mathematicians! Today, we're going on a journey through the world of numbers and rings to better understand one special type of number - prime numbers. You might think primes are just those whole numbers greater than 1 that can only be divided by themselves and 1, but there's more to uncover! Imagine a magical toy box containing different types of toys called "elements." These elements can be grouped into various boxes or collections called "special rings," each having its own unique rules. In these special rings, instead of multiplying regular whole numbers, we combine things called polynomials. Don't worry; polynomials are just expressions made up of variables and coefficients, like 3x^2 + 2x - 5. Now let's focus on a particular special ring, filled with polynomial integers - expressions involving x and integer coefficients. Think of them as building blocks that follow specific combining rules. Some building blocks cannot be broken down any further - meet indivisible blocks, similar to prime numbers! They share many characteristics with primes, making them perfect for studying their behavior. In these special rings, indivisible blocks behave similarly to prime numbers: when an indivisible block divides a combination of blocks without leaving any remainder, it plays the role of a prime number divisor within the special ring. Just like normal prime numbers, indivisible blocks don't divide any other blocks except themselves, 1, or combinations that include multiples of them. Let's explore an exciting theorem about indivisible blocks in a Principal Ideal Domain (PID), another fancy name for a specific kind of special ring: Theorem: An indivisible block in a PID acts exactly like a prime number. This means that whenever an indivisible block divides a product of blocks, it must divide at least one factor. It's just like saying if a prime number (indivisible block) is a factor of a certain product of numbers (block combination), then it has to be a factor of either one or both of the original numbers (blocks). Neat, isn't it? By learning about primes using these fascinating special rings and their indivisible blocks, you get a deeper understanding of why primes behave differently from other numbers. So next time you come across a prime number puzzle, remember your friends - the indivisible blocks living in special rings! Um there's one other item that you didn't mention. [Nina and her mother had gathered plants and crossed a bridge during their hike.] Title: How to Have Fun While Watching a Movie Watching movies can be an enjoyable experience, whether you're alone or with company. By following these steps, you can enhance your movie-watching experience and have fun while doing so. ### Step 1: Choose the Right Film Before watching a movie, selecting the right film is essential. Consider factors such as genre, length, ratings, and reviews when choosing a movie. This will help ensure that you watch something you enjoy and minimize the risk of boredom or disappointment. Key tip: If you are watching with others, consider their preferences too. A consensus decision often leads to a more positive viewing experience. ### Step 2: Create a Comfortable Environment A comfortable environment contributes significantly to having fun during a movie. Adjust lighting conditions (dimming lights), arrange seating comfortably, and maintain a moderate room temperature. Additionally, reduce background noise by turning off electronic devices like radios and televisions. Guideline: Make sure all viewers agree on the setting; this prevents distractions and enhances focus on the movie. ### Step 3: Prepare Snacks and Drinks Having snacks and drinks at hand makes the movie time feel more festive. Opt for finger foods that don't require utensils for easy eating. Popcorn, chips, candies, nuts, fruits, pizza slices, soft drinks, juices, water, coffee, or tea are excellent choices. Remember, overindulging might lead to discomfort, negatively impacting your overall enjoyment. Key tip: Avoid messy foods that could spill and potentially damage electronics. ### Step 4: Familiarize Yourself With Equipment Being tech-savvy about your home theater system ensures smooth operation throughout the movie. Understand how to adjust volume levels, switch inputs, navigate menus, pause, rewind, or fast forward if necessary. Knowing these functions allows uninterrupted viewings and quick resolution should issues arise. ### Step 5: Engage Actively With The Movie Active engagement involves paying attention to details, characters, plot twists, dialogue, visual effects, music scores, etc., within the movie. Laugh along with comedic moments, gasp at suspenseful scenes, express shock at surprising turns - let yourself get immersed in the storyline. Active participation heightens emotional responses which contribute to a memorable movie night. ### Step 6: Share Opinions And Experiences Discuss the movie with fellow viewers during intermissions or after the credits roll. Sharing opinions fosters connections among viewers, creates talking points, and promotes critical thinking skills. You may discuss aspects like acting performances, special effects, cinematography, soundtrack, message, etc. Respect everyone's views even if they differ from yours. ### Step 7: Play Games Related To The Movie Games related to the movie theme can add another layer of excitement. For example, trivia quizzes based on the movie genre, predicting what happens next, or spotting continuity errors can make the entire process interactive and engaging. These activities also test your observation skills and knowledge about films. ### Step 8: Use Social Media Platforms Tweet about the movie using relevant hashtags, update your status on Facebook, or share your thoughts through Instagram stories. Interacting online lets you connect with other fans, read diverse perspectives, and sometimes access exclusive content related to the film. However, avoid excessive phone usage since it distracts from fully enjoying the movie itself. By incorporating these steps into your movie routine, you'll find yourself having much more fun while watching movies! Once upon a time there was a boy called Jake. [S squeaks at 6.36; 6.4 S makes fussy noise which D echos] Title: How to Get Free Music on an iPhone Introduction: While there are many music streaming services available today that require a monthly or annual subscription fee, such as Apple Music and Spotify Premium, you can still get free music on your iPhone without breaking any laws. This tutorial will guide you through several methods to obtain ad-supported songs, radio stations, and podcasts from various sources directly onto your device. Method 1: Using Built-in Apps iPhones come with preinstalled apps like Apple Music, Radio, and Podcasts that allow users to access free content. Here's how: Step 1: Open the Apple Music app This is where you'll find millions of songs, playlists curated by editors, and internet radio stations. Although some features require payment, you can indeed discover and enjoy plenty of tunes at no cost. Key Tip: Tap 'Radio' > 'Apple Music 1' to listen to live broadcasts hosted by renowned DJs around the clock. You may also stumble upon interviews with famous artists! Step 2: Browse 'For You', 'New', and 'Radio' tabs Under these sections, you'll see recommendations based on genres and artists you like. Moreover, new releases and popular tracks are regularly updated here. Guideline: Remember, if you want to save a song for offline listening, consider adding it to your library. However, this feature requires an active Apple Music subscription. Method 2: Utilizing Third-Party Applications Several third-party applications provide free music downloads legally. One such example is SoundCloud. Step 1: Download and Install SoundCloud from App Store SoundCloud hosts user-uploaded content including remixes, covers, and original compositions. Many creators offer their music for free here. Step 2: Sign up or Log in To download tracks, you need to create an account first. It's straightforward - just enter your email address, preferred password, and birthdate. Alternatively, log in via Facebook or Google. Step 3: Search for Tracks Use the search bar located at the top of the screen to look for specific songs, albums, or artists. Step 4: Identify Downloaded-Enabled Songs Not all songs on SoundCloud can be downloaded due to copyright restrictions. Look for the download button (a cloud icon with a downward arrow) next to a track to confirm its availability. Step 5: Save Track for Offline Listening Tap the download button beside the chosen song. Once completed, a small green tick mark will appear over the cloud icon indicating successful download. To locate your saved tracks, go to 'Library' tab then 'Tracks'. Additional Tips: - Keep your iOS version updated to ensure compatibility with different apps. - Be cautious when choosing which third-party apps to install. Always read reviews and check ratings beforehand. - Never engage in piracy; respect artist rights and avoid using illegal platforms offering unlimited free music downloads. Conclusion: With patience and persistence, getting free music on your iPhone doesn't have to be complicated nor compromising. By utilizing built-in apps and trusted third-party providers, you can build a diverse collection of songs, stay current with trending hits, and support emerging talents along the way. A "camera" is, in its simplest form, like a box with a hole in front of it. There is a special piece of glass in front of the hole called a lens. To take a photograph of something, the lens makes a small picture of the object inside the box. The lens does this by focusing light. A lens in a camera works like a lens in glasses (spectacles) or a magnifying glass. One type of camera called the Pinhole camera has no lens but uses a very small hole to focus light. Germs are everywhere - in the air and on everything. They just are too small to see with your eyes. There are four kinds of germs. They come in many shapes and sizes. Bacteria and virus germs are the ones that usually make you sick. If bad bacteria get into your blood, it will give off poisons. When enough poison is in your blood, you will get sick. You may get a headache, ear ache, stomach ache or a cavity in your tooth. Medicine helps kill the bacteria and makes you feel better. Virus invades your cells. Virus causes colds, the flu, measles and many other diseases. There are no medicines to kill a virus. But, there are medicines that help protect you from getting viruses - they are called vaccines. Some viruses can live for a while on something like a doorknob, so wash your hands often! Germs enter your body through a cut or scratch, through your nose, your eyes and your mouth. If germs get in your body, the white blood cells in your body fight the germs and try to kill them. But sometimes the germs are too tough. In a couple hours, they can multiply into a million germs! How do doctors figure out what is making you sick? They look at samples of your blood, saliva or other fluids under a microscope. The doctor can tell which kind of germ you have and how it is making you sick. If you are sick, it is best to eat healthy foods, drink lots of water and get lots of rest. These help your body fight the germs and help you feel better faster. Check out our "Tips to Stay Healthy" page for more germ-fighting tips. Will you come upstairs and play He mans with me? Title: How to Dry a Salt Shaker That You Really Need to Use Right Away Introduction: Have you ever found yourself in a situation where your salt shaker is wet or has some moisture inside, making it impossible to dispense salt? This can be quite inconvenient when you need to add seasoning to your food immediately. Fortunately, there are several ways to dry out a salt shaker quickly so that you can put salt in right away. Here's our comprehensive guide on how to do just that! **Step 1: Empty the Salt Shaker** Before starting the drying process, ensure all remaining damp salt granules are removed from the shaker. Dump any visible water droplets as well. An empty shaker will expedite the overall drying time significantly. Key Tip: If possible, rinse the interior with warm water and mild dish soap then let it air-dry overnight if you don't need it urgently. Make sure to dry thoroughly before refilling with salt. **Step 2: Absorb Excess Moisture** Use a clean paper towel or cloth to absorb as much excess moisture as possible. Pay special attention to areas around the holes at the top of the shaker since these are most likely causing the clogging issue. Be gentle during this step to avoid pushing the wet salt further into the tiny openings. Guideline: It might take multiple attempts to completely clear off the moisture depending on its extent. Patience is key here. **Step 3: Utilize Rice Trick (Optional)**: If the first two steps didn't work effectively, try filling the shaker halfway with uncooked rice grains. The rice acts as a desiccant by absorbing residual moisture within the container. Let it sit for about an hour before proceeding to the next step. Important Note: Remember to remove the rice after usage; otherwise, it could mix with the salt affecting both taste and texture. **Step 4: Heat Treatment (Recommended Only For Glass Shakers)** For glass salt shakers, consider using heat treatment to speed up evaporation. Place the shaker (without lid preferably) near a heating vent or a sunny window sill for approximately 30 minutes. However, exercise caution while doing this as excessive heat may cause damage to certain types of plastic shakers. Cautionary Advice: Never leave unattended near direct flames or high temperatures to prevent potential fire hazards or melting. **Step 5: Test Dispensability** Shake the shaker gently over a plate to check whether salt flows smoothly through the dispenser. Repeat previous steps if necessary until optimal functionality resumes. **Step 6: Refill With Fresh Salt** Once the shaker operates correctly again, feel free to fill it up with fresh salt ready for immediate use. Conclusion: A little patience and creativity go a long way in handling everyday problems like a wet salt shaker. By following these simple yet effective methods outlined above, you should now have a fully functional salt shaker ready for instant utilization. Enjoy adding flavor to your dishes without delay! Bath is where Roald Dahl's short story "The Landlady" takes place. The city is also mentioned in many of Jane Austen's books, like "Northanger Abbey", "Sense and Sensibility", and "Pride and Prejudice." White dwarfs cool slowly for two reasons. They have an extremely small surface area to radiate this heat from, so they cool gradually, remaining hot for a long time. Also, they are very opaque. The degenerate matter that makes up the bulk of a white dwarf stops light and other electromagnetic radiation, so radiation does not carry away much energy. Your bones are tough stuff - but even tough stuff can break. Like a wooden pencil, bones will bend under strain. But if the pressure is too much, or too sudden, bones can snap. You can break a bone by falling off a skateboard or crashing down from the monkey bars. When a bone breaks it is called a fracture (say: FRAK-chur). There's more than one way to break or fracture a bone. A break can be anything from a hairline fracture (a thin break in the bone) to the bone that's snapped in two pieces like a broken tree branch. Doctors describe fractures in the following ways: A complete fracture is when the bone has broken into two pieces. A greenstick fracture is when the bone cracks on one side only, not all the way through. A single fracture is when the bone is broken in one place. A comminuted (say: kah-muh-NOOT-ed) fracture is when the bone is broken into more than two pieces or crushed. A bowing fracture, which only happens in kids, is when the bone bends but doesn't break. An open fracture is when the bone is sticking through the skin. It hurts to break a bone! It's different for everyone, but the pain is often like the deep ache you get from a super bad stomachache or headache. Some people may experience sharper pain - especially with an open fracture. And if the fracture is small, a kid may not feel much pain at all. Sometimes, kids won't even be able to tell that they broke a bone! Breaking a bone is a big shock to your whole body. It's normal for you to receive strong messages from parts of your body that aren't anywhere close to the fracture. You may feel dizzy, woozy, or chilly from the shock. A lot of people cry for a while. Some people pass out until their bodies have time to adjust to all the signals they're getting. And other people don't feel any pain right away because of the shock of the injury. If you think you or someone else has broken a bone, the most important things to do are to: make sure the person who is hurt is as comfortable as possible tell an adult if there are no adults around, call 911 or the emergency number in your area The worst thing for a broken bone is to move it. This will hurt the person and it can make the injury worse! In the case of a broken arm or leg, a grown-up may be able to cushion or support the surrounding area with towels or pillows. One super-important tip: If you're not sure what bone is broken or you think the neck or back is broken, do not try to move the injured person. Wait until a trained medical professional has arrived! What Does the Doctor Do? To treat the broken bone, the doctor needs to know which kind of fracture it is. That's where X-rays come in handy. X-rays are like pictures that give doctors a map of fractures so that they can set the bones back in their normal position. After your bone has been set, the next step is usually putting on a cast, the special bandage that will keep the bone in place for the 1 to 2 months it will take for the break to mend. Casts are made of bandages soaked in plaster, which harden to a tough shell (that's why they last so long!). Sometimes casts are made of fiberglass or plastic - and some are even waterproof, which means you can still go swimming and get them wet! And sometimes they come in cool colors or patterns that you can choose. With breaks in larger bones or when a bone breaks in more than two pieces, the doctor may need to put in a metal pin - or pins - to help set it. For this operation, you'll get some medicine so you'll be asleep and unable to feel any pain. When your bone has healed, the doctor will remove the pin or pins. Your bones are natural healers. At the location of the fracture, your bones will produce lots of new cells and tiny blood vessels that rebuild the bone. These cells cover both ends of the broken part of the bone and close up the break until it's as good as new. What to Do When the Cast Comes Off Can you believe they use a saw to remove your cast? The funny thing is this saw doesn't hurt your skin at all. It might even tickle! Once the cast is off, the injured area will probably look and feel pretty weird. The body part that was in a cast might look strange at first. The skin might be pale, dry, or flaky. Body hair might look darker and the body part itself might look smaller because you might have lost some muscle while it was healing. Don't worry. This is all temporary. Kids are great healers, so you'll be back to normal soon. In some cases, your doctor might suggest you do special exercises to improve your strength and flexibility. You'll want to go slow and ask the doctor if you should avoid any activities, such as hanging from the monkey bars. If you want to return to a sport, ask the doctor how soon you'll be able to do it. How can you be sure you don't break any more bones? Accidents happen, but you often can prevent injuries by wearing safety helmets, pads, and the right protective gear for your activity or sport. It's also a smart idea to do what you can to build strong bones. How do you do that? Get a lot of physical activity, especially stuff like jumping and running. Feed your bones the calcium and vitamin D they need to stay strong. That means getting your share of milk and other calcium-rich foods and drinks, such as broccoli and calcium-fortified orange juice. He had a lot of beautiful colors. Various organizations have long recognized the importance of construction activities that may damage tree health. This can result in monetary losses due to tree damage and replacement costs. As a result, standard methods of tree management for building activities are well established and tested. yeah, I have never seen them like that before. Mercury can dissolve large amounts of aluminium metal, making it dangerous to transport in aluminium containers. The thin layer of oxide on aluminium stops it from amalgamating (making an amalgam with) aluminium, but the oxide coating can be damaged to expose the metal. Then the aluminium metal is dissolved and oxidizes to aluminium oxide. The aluminium oxide forms a solid and releases the mercury, which amalgamates more aluminium. This process keeps repeating until a large amount of aluminium is dissolved. Now get in your chair and eat okay? yeah, you have to be bigger to use a knife. oh, you're such a big girl now. Um a cookie and the peanut butter. Title: How to Cancel Boomerang on Android Boomerang is a popular application that allows users to send scheduled messages or emails at a later time. However, if you no longer wish to use this service, you may want to cancel your subscription. This tutorial will guide you through the process of cancelling your Boomerang subscription on an Android device. Step 1: Open the Google Play Store app The first step is to open the Google Play Store app on your Android device. The Google Play Store is where you download and manage all of your apps, including Boomerang. By opening the app, you'll be able to access your subscriptions and make changes to them. Step 2: Tap the hamburger menu icon Once you have opened the Google Play Store app, locate the three horizontal lines (also known as the hamburger menu) in the upper left-hand corner of the screen. Tapping on this icon will reveal a sidebar with various options. Step 3: Select "Subscriptions" From the list of options displayed in the sidebar, tap on "Subscriptions." This option will take you to a page displaying all of the active subscriptions associated with your Google account. Step 4: Find Boomerang in your list of subscriptions Scroll down until you find Boomerang in your list of subscriptions. It should appear alphabetically under the letter 'B.' Once you've located it, tap its name to proceed. Step 5: Tap "Cancel Subscription" Upon tapping on Boomerang, you will be taken to a new page containing details about your subscription, such as renewal dates and payment methods. At the bottom of this page, there should be a button labeled "Cancel Subscription." Go ahead and tap it. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Make sure you are signed into the correct Google account before attempting to cancel your subscription. If you have multiple accounts, double-check by looking at the email address shown in the top right corner of the screen. * Be aware that uninstalling the Boomerang app from your device does not automatically cancel your subscription. You must complete these steps within the Google Play Store app to successfully cancel your subscription. * Keep in mind that after cancellation, you will still have access to Boomerang features until the end of your current billing cycle. Afterward, your subscription will expire, and you won't be billed again unless you choose to resubscribe. Summary: To cancel Boomerang on Android, open the Google Play Store app, navigate to your subscriptions, select Boomerang, and then tap "Cancel Subscription." Confirm any prompts and ensure you are using the correct Google account. Remember, you will retain access until the end of the current billing period. For example, some Christians and some Muslims believe that swearing is a sin. Mommy it's not time for you to come out. Susie Derkins, the only character in the strip with both first and last names, is a classmate of Calvin who lives in his neighborhood. She first appeared early in the strip as a new student in Calvin's class. Unlike Calvin, she is polite and very smart and eager to improve in her studies and grades at school, and her imagination usually seems mild-mannered and calm, consisting of a standard young girls' games such as playing house or having tea parties with her stuffed animals. Her approach to these games is arguably more modern, however, some might say even skeptical. (In a game of "house" she usually casts herself as the traditional working wife while Calvin is the lousy and useless husband or some version similar to that.) "Derkins" was the nickname of Watterson's wife's family beagle, and he liked the name so much he named this character after it. As much as either of them hate to admit, Calvin and Susie have quite a bit in common. (Susie is shown on occasion with a stuffed rabbit known as "Mr. Bun," and Calvin always has Hobbes.) Title: How to Make a Popping Sound with Your Mouth Without Using Your Fingers Introduction: Making a popping sound with your mouth without using your fingers can be an entertaining skill to learn and show off to friends and family. This technique is often used in beatboxing, singing, or simply as a fun party trick. While it might seem challenging at first, with practice and patience, you can master this skill. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to do it: Step 1: Understand the Basics The popping sound is created when air from your lungs rushes through a small opening in your lips, creating a sudden burst that results in a distinct "pop" noise. The challenge lies in controlling the airflow and lip shape to produce this effect consistently. Step 2: Relax Your Facial Muscles Before attempting any sounds, relax all facial muscles, especially those around your mouth and jaw. A tense face will restrict the necessary movements required for making the popping sound. Practice yawning or smiling widely to loosen up these areas. Step 3: Form a Small Circle with Your Lips Pucker your lips as if preparing to whistle but keep them loose enough so they form more of an 'O' shape rather than tightly pursed together. Try to create a circle about the size of a dime or smaller; the smaller the hole, the higher pitched the resulting pop will be. Step 4: Adjust Air Pressure Take a deep breath and slowly build up air pressure behind your closed lips. Be careful not to overdo it - excessive force could lead to discomfort or even injury. Instead, focus on maintaining steady control over the amount of air you're pushing out. Step 5: Release the Air Quickly Suddenly release the built-up air by parting your lips just briefly before quickly closing them again. Timing is crucial here; too short or long may result in no sound or merely a hissing one. It should feel like snapping your fingers but with your lips instead. Step 6: Repeat and Refine Like any new skill, repetition is key. Keep practicing until you find the right balance between lip shape, air pressure, and timing. Don't get discouraged if you don't succeed immediately; remember, consistency comes with time and effort. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Start slow: Begin by whispering "puh," paying close attention to the feeling of air escaping through your lips. Gradually increase volume and speed once you become comfortable with the sensation. - Use visual cues: Watch yourself perform in a mirror or record yourself to analyze what works and what doesn't. Seeing yourself execute the movement can provide valuable insights into improving your technique. - Experiment with variations: Once you've mastered the basic pop, try experimenting with different shapes and sizes of your oral cavity (mouth) to alter pitch and tone. - Stay hydrated: Dry lips make consistent popping more difficult. Drinking plenty of water throughout the day ensures your lips stay moisturized and pliable. Conclusion: With persistence and dedication, anyone can learn to make a popping sound with their mouth without needing to use their fingers. As you progress, incorporate this newfound talent into various aspects of performance art such as beatboxing, vocal percussion, or impressions. Most importantly, enjoy the journey of learning something novel! You know before I would have said books. I need ta go for a walk, another one. oh, Chi! you do not know what you're missing. Collinearity: Lining Up Points on One Straight Line --------------------------------------------------- Imagine you have three friends, Alice, Ben, and Cathy, standing along a straight path. Even though they are spread out along the path, we can say they are "collinear," because they all line up on one single straight line. In math, we can represent the position of our friends using points, just like dots on a map. These points can be written down as coordinates (x, y), where x represents how far along the line they are, and y shows how high or low they are compared to the starting point. For example, let's say Alice is at position (1, 2), Ben is at (3, 7), and poor Cathy is way back at (-3, -5). We need to figure out if they are collinear-all lined up on one straight line. To do this, we will subtract the positions of each pair of friends and see whether their differences follow a pattern. First, let's find the difference between Ben and Alice: Ben - Alice = (3, 7) - (1, 2) = (2, 5) Next, let's find the difference between Cathy and Ben: Cathy - Ben = (-3, -5) - (3, 7) = (-6, -12) Notice anything interesting? The second pair of differences is exactly twice the first pair! This means that no matter where our friends are along the line, they are indeed collinear since their position differences form a constant ratio. Just like Alice, Ben, and Cathy lining up along a straight path, these mathematical points also share the same property when it comes to their relative distances. In real life, there are many situations involving collinearity. Have you ever arranged your toys in a row, or placed sticks side by side to build a bridge? All those items lie perfectly aligned, following the concept of being collinear! Scientists must be very careful to make explanations that fit well with what they observe and measure. They compete to provide better explanations. An explanation might be interesting or pleasing, but if it does not agree with what other scientists really see and measure, they will try to find a better explanation. Some scientists teach at universities and other places and train people to become scientists. Scientists often do experiments to find out more about reality, and sometimes may repeat experiments or use control groups. Scientists who are doing applied science try to use scientific knowledge to improve the world. They followed the shore until they came to their canoe. Smart Helen Hefferan Elem.School 4409 W. Wilcox St. Chicago IL 60624 This assignment will be for 6th grade students. The main objective of this mini- teach is to show the difference between Work and Torque, in relationship to Force and Distance. 1. A Jensen balance bar with ten 50 gram weights. 2. A Torque bar with a 500g weight. 3. A wooden wheel with four nuts and bolts drilled in it. 1. The students are given the weight and distance on the left side of the balance bar and asked where to place another weight in order to balance 2. Give the student the torque bar and a weight. The teacher moves the weights to different positions on the bar. Record the student's 3. Have some students use a small wrench to loosen 2 bolts and other students use a large wrench to loosen 2 bolts. Record the observations. Discuss the ratio between amount of force and the length of the wrench. (The longer the wrench, the less force required to loosen the bolts.) Assessment will be based on the following: 1. The teacher will put a weight on the balance bar at a specific location and give the student a weight. The student will be asked to balance the bar by choosing the appropriate location on the bar. 2. Torque worksheet with the formula, (T = D x F). 3. The student will explain why the larger wrench loosens the nut with less force than the smaller wrench. 1. The torque on both sides of a Jensen Arm, when the bar is balanced, will 2. The bar will require more force in order to hold it horizontally as the weight is moved away from the handle. (It will feel "heavier", but it is the SAME MASS, only in a different location. It requires MORE FORCE, but is no heavier.) 3. The larger wrench will remove the nuts with less force than the smaller wrench. (D x f = d x F) (The large wrench, a BIG DISTANCE times a small force is equal to a small distance times a BIG FORCE, the small wrench.) 4. The difference between torque and work is the concept of rotation. Torque is work (distance times force) which causes rotation. Once upon a time, there were two brothers who lived in the same house. She was deft at mending glass and china, and equally so at mending all sorts of household things. She darned the napkins and tablecloths in a way that excited my mother's admiration, and was always so obliging and ready to offer her services that, in time, a resort to Mary's work-basket and ever ready needle became the most natural thing in the world to all of us. Make sure it stands out by customizing its appearance with unique clothing, accessories, and colors. This will make it easier for other users to recognize and remember you. * Key Tip: Regularly update your outfit to keep up with the latest fashion trends in Woozworld. This gives dedicated followers a place to connect, share ideas, and support one another. As the founder, you'll serve as a leader and role model, further solidifying your status within the community. * Key Tip: Stay actively involved in your fan club by posting updates, organizing events, and acknowledging members' contributions. ### Step 6: Collaborate With Other Users Partnering with popular Woozen (Woozworld users) can significantly increase your exposure. Look for opportunities to collaborate on projects, host joint events, or simply offer support and encouragement. These connections can lead to valuable friendships and partnerships. * Guideline: Choose collaboration partners carefully. Sharing your talents not only demonstrates your creativity but also provides entertainment for others. You may even inspire fellow Woozens to develop their own abilities. * Key Tip: Offer constructive feedback and praise to those who share their talents with you. Encouraging others promotes positivity and strengthens relationships. By following these steps and consistently engaging with the Woozworld community, you'll gradually build a strong presence and become a recognized figure within the game. Just remember to always prioritize kindness, respect, and honesty along the way. Title: How to Change the Registered Name in Windows Windows is a popular operating system used by millions of people around the world. When you first install Windows, you are prompted to enter a registered name or user account name. This name appears every time you log into your computer and can be seen by other users who access your machine. If you want to change this registered name for any reason, follow these steps: **Step 1: Access User Accounts Settings** Click the Start button (or press the Windows key) and select the gear icon to open the Settings app. Alternatively, you can type "Settings" in the search bar and click on the corresponding result. Once the Settings window opens, find and click on the "Accounts" option. *Key tip*: Make sure you have administrative privileges before attempting to make changes to your user account settings. You may need to provide an administrator password during the process. **Step 2: Navigate to Your User Account** In the left-hand pane of the Accounts window, click on "Family & other users." Here, you will see all the accounts associated with your PC. Find your primary account - usually labeled as "Administrator" or similar - and click on it. *Guideline*: Changing the registered name affects only the display name; it does not alter your username or affect files' ownership or permissions. **Step 3: Edit the Display Name** After clicking on your account, look for the "Account name" field under your profile picture. Click on the existing name, delete it, and then replace it with the new one. After typing the new name, hit Enter or click outside the text box to save the changes. *Key tip*: The new name should adhere to standard naming conventions and avoid special characters or sensitive words that might conflict with system processes. **Step 4: Confirm Changes** You will now be asked to sign out and sign back in to confirm the changes. Save any work in progress and close all running applications. Then click "Sign out now" when prompted. Log back in using your password, and you should now see the updated registered name displayed. *Guideline*: To ensure seamless operation, restart your computer after changing the registered name. This action refreshes the system configuration and eliminates potential conflicts arising from modified account details. By following these steps, you have successfully changed the registered name in Windows. Remember that modifying user account settings requires caution, so always double-check your actions and maintain regular backups to protect important data. How do you get back to school? Mm [yes] there all the numbers over ten right. A Quick Color Explanation It's pretty easy to understand the words color codes, but those hex codes are rather strange. Here's a quick explanation. The three primary colors are red, blue, and yellow. Remember that from high school art? They're called the primary colors because there are no two "lesser" colors that make them up. Purple is not a primary color because it can be created by combining equal parts of blue and red. In the world of mechanical things that make color, like a television or a computer screen, color is created by mixing three basic colors to make other colors. It's a process known as "additive color." You would think that the TVs and computer monitors of the world would simply use the three primary colors to start with, but nothing in life can be that easy. The three colors used to start additive color mixing are red, green, and blue. Why, you ask? Because by starting with one composite color, green, you can still create yellow, because it's contained in the green. In addition, now you are actually starting with four colors, red, green, blue, yellow. Stay with me here.... To go on, I need to explain a second process of working with colors: "subtractive color." Subtractive color is the concept of combining colors to make another, like mixing red and blue to get purple. That may sound like additive color, but in reality, colors are made by subtracting a hue out of the color scheme by adding more of another. Adding more white to black makes it more silver, subtracting more black as more white is mixed in. Get it? One other big difference between additive and subtractive color, and this is the key, is what you get when you add them all together. If you add all the colors together in a subtractive color method, you get black. Why? Because you added them all together and all those colors subtracted from all the others leaving no set color... or black. A computer, on the other hand, works with light, not paint or any other goopy stuff. Mix a computer's additive colors -- red, green, and blue -- and you get white. No kidding, you really do. Shine a white light at a prism or a lead crystal glass. You'll get a rainbow of colors. Actually, that's how a rainbow is created. White light is being shown through water in the air. That separates the white light into the "rainbow" of colors. Now on to the six-digit representation of color known as the hex code. Basic Hexadecimal Notation: Zero, "0", is the smallest representations of a color. It's almost the total absence of color. F is 15 times the intensity of the color of 0. Combinations of these digits create different shades of a particular color. Double zero, "00," is equal to zero hue. FF is equal to a pure color. This color representation is done three times, once for red, once for green, and once for blue, in that order. Put the three, two-digit codes together and you get a six-digit hex code. The hex code is just a representation of the red, green, and blue intensity, in that order. The computer creates the three intensities, mashes them together, and you get a single shade of color. For an example, here are the opposite ends of the color scale: The code above is equal to white. Why? Notice the three colors are all set to FF. That means the highest level of red, green, and blue. As I said above, in a computer or television, the combination of all three primary colors creates white. Now here's black: This is just the opposite. All three settings of red, green, and blue are set to a total absence of color. Black. Now, here are a few other codes and their breakdowns: Let's start with the concern from up above, yellow. The code above produces pure yellow. Notice the red and the green are at full tilt. There is no blue. By mashing the red and green up against each other, the red cancels out the blue and all that is left is the yellow. It's actually a subtractive color method being employed in an additive world. Man, this gets loopy, huh? The code above creates a shade of red called "crimson." The red setting, DC, is pretty intense. There's not much green. Blue is set a little less than halfway up. That's violet. The red and the blue are at pretty high levels. The green is there, but at a lower level. Now, this is not purple, but violet. Purple, as I said above, is a combination of red and blue alone. The code is 800080. Notice there's no green at all. Just an equal amount of red and blue. That's orange. There's lots of red, not quite an equal level of green, and no blue. That's how the hex codes work. This lesson probably won't make you an expert in color creation, but at least you'll be able to understand the creation of color in a computer. So, are there more hex code colors that what I show above? Oh, yes. There are thousands upon thousands covering every color in the scale from pure black to pure white. Every time you change even one of the red, green, or blue levels, you change the color. Feel free to change the colors to get exactly the shade you want. Push uh got it boy that's hard to do. [he always wants to talk about Star Wars.] The word "electricity" is sometimes used to mean "electrical energy". They are not the same thing: electricity is a transmission medium for electrical energy, like sea water is a transmission medium for wave energy. An item which allows electricity to move through it is called a conductor. Copper wires and other metal items are good conductors, allowing electricity to move through them and transmit electrical energy. Plastic is a bad conductor (also called an insulator) and doesn't allow much electricity to move through it so it will stop the transmission of electrical energy. "Ah! [MOT looks up to see TC holding red, yellow and green ball that are stuck together.] [MOT and Chi are pointing at pictures] |Make Your Own |You can use numbers to draw your own math cats! Here's how: Print out a grid and a chart with ordered pairs of numbers, such as (4,2). In each ordered pair, the first number tells you how far left or right to mark your point. The second number tells you how far up or down to mark your point. Plot each point on the grid. Draw a line from each point to the next to make a math cat. | Math Cat #1 uses one quadrant. 0 is at the bottom left corner. To plot each point, first move to the right, then up. Math Cat #2 uses 4 quadrants. | 0 is in the center. To plot each point, first move right or left, then up or down. A negative number (such as -2) is less than 0. If the first number is negative, move to the left of 0. If the second number is negative, move below 0. You'll also need this 4 quadrant grid. | copyright 2001 - by Wendy Petti of Math Cats. Title: How to Get Out of Eating Vegetables as a Kid Introduction: Eating vegetables can be a challenging task for many kids. If you're looking for ways to avoid eating your veggies without causing unnecessary conflict or harm, this guide will provide you with some strategies. However, keep in mind that consuming a balanced diet rich in fruits and vegetables is essential for good health and growth. This tutorial aims to help you navigate through mealtimes while encouraging healthier eating habits in the long run. Step 1: Understand Your Dislike for Vegetables Before attempting any strategy, take time to understand why you dislike vegetables. Common reasons include taste, texture, or appearance. Recognizing these factors will allow you to tailor your approach accordingly and communicate effectively with parents or caregivers about your preferences. Key Tips: - Be honest about your feelings towards different types of vegetables. - Avoid labeling all vegetables as "yucky" - focus on specific ones instead. Step 2: Explore Alternative Preparation Methods Sometimes, the way vegetables are prepared can significantly impact their taste and texture. Experiment with various cooking techniques like roasting, grilling, steaming, or saut ing to find methods that make vegetables more appealing to you. Key Guidelines: - Research recipes online or ask family members for ideas. - Always ensure proper cooking times to maintain nutritional value. Step 3: Hide Vegetables in Other Foods Incorporate finely chopped or pureed vegetables into dishes such as pasta sauces, casseroles, or smoothies. By disguising them within other ingredients, you may not even notice they're there! Key Tips: - Start by adding small amounts of vegetables and gradually increase over time. - Opt for milder-tasting vegetables (e.g., carrots, zucchini) when beginning this method. Step 4: Negotiate With Parents or Caregivers Have an open conversation with your parents or caregivers about your vegetable concerns. Propose alternatives or compromises, such as trying one new vegetable per week or finding creative ways to incorporate them into meals together. Key Guidelines: - Approach the discussion respectfully and calmly. - Offer suggestions rather than demands. Step 5: Gradual Exposure Slowly introduce yourself to vegetables by starting with those that have mild flavors and familiar textures. Over time, expand your palate to try less familiar options. Key Tips: - Begin with cooked vegetables before transitioning to raw ones if needed. - Pair new vegetables with dips or sauces that complement their flavor profile. Step 6: Educate Yourself About Nutrition Learn about the benefits of individual vegetables and how they contribute to overall health. Gaining knowledge about nutrition can motivate you to consume a well-rounded diet. Key Guidelines: - Utilize reputable sources (e.g., government health websites, educational resources) for information. - Share what you learn with your parents or caregivers to encourage conversations around healthy eating habits. Conclusion: While getting out of eating vegetables as a kid might seem like an impossible feat, understanding your own tastes and exploring alternative preparation methods can lead to discovering new favorites. Remember always to prioritize open communication with parents or caregivers and strive for gradual exposure to various foods. Ultimately, developing a positive relationship with food sets the foundation for lifelong healthy eating habits. Once upon a time there were two friends, Jack and Jill. There are many additional features to the existing mac-OS apps. Some highlights are Dark Mode (UI theme & appearance), time shifts reflecting the hour of the day (desktop background), file stacks, edit features in quick look, continuity camera and Group FaceTime features also adds the screenshot app. How Natural Gas Is Made The natural gas that we use to heat our homes and our water comes from deep under the earth. The gas is found in layers of rock with tiny holes - the rock holds the gas like a sponge. To bring it to the surface, gas companies drill down hundreds of feet and pump into pipes. But how did the natural gas get there? You already know that dinosaurs roamed the earth millions of years ago. When the dinosaurs and the plants they ate died, the remains were buried under many layers of rock and soil. Over time, the tremendous heat and pressure created by the layers of earth turned the animal and plant matter into natural gas and petroleum (oil). That's why natural gas is called a "fossil fuel." Want to learn more? If you'd like to learn more about natural gas, check out Energy Kids. Title: How to Accept a Non-Member Parent on Club Penguin Club Penguin is a popular online multiplayer game that allows players to interact with other penguins in a virtual world filled with fun activities and social interactions. While the game has many features available to members only, non-members can still enjoy certain aspects of the game. If you're a member of Club Penguin and would like to add a non-member parent as a friend or buddy, this tutorial will guide you through the process step by step. Step 1: Log into Your Account To start, log in to your Club Penguin account using your username and password. Make sure you have full access to all member features before proceeding. Key Tip: Keep your login information secure and do not share it with anyone, including parents. This ensures your safety while playing the game. Step 2: Access Your Buddy List Once logged in, look at the top right corner of the screen for the icon that looks like a person with a plus sign (+) next to it. Click on this icon to open your buddy list. Guideline: The buddy list is where you can see all your friends and buddies currently online. It also serves as an easy way to communicate and play games together. Step 3: Prepare to Add a New Friend At the bottom of your buddy list, there are two options - "Add Buddy" and "Find Buddies." Since you want to add a specific non-member parent, click on "Add Buddy." A text box will appear asking for their penguin name. Step 4: Enter the Parent's Penguin Name Type in the exact penguin name of the non-member parent you wish to add as a buddy. Double check for spelling errors and capitalization since usernames are case sensitive. Once entered correctly, press "OK." Key Tip: Be cautious when adding people as buddies. Only add those whom you know personally or trust. Remember, if someone seems suspicious or makes you uncomfortable, report them immediately to prevent potential issues. Step 5: Wait for Confirmation After sending the request, wait for the non-member parent to accept your invitation. They should receive a notification both in-game and via email (if they provided one during registration). Until they confirm, their status will show up as "pending" on your buddy list. Guideline: Depending on whether the recipient is currently online or not, accepting your invite may take some time. Patience is key here! Step 6: Verify Their Acceptance Check back periodically to see if the non-member parent has accepted your friend request. When they do, their status on your buddy list changes from "pending" to "buddy," indicating successful addition. Key Tip: You can now chat with your new buddy using predefined phrases found within the game's messaging system. Always adhere to the rules set forth by Disney regarding appropriate language and behavior. Congratulations! By following these steps, you've successfully added a non-member parent as a buddy on Club Penguin. Enjoy chatting, playing games, and exploring the wondrous world of Club Penguin together! yeah, you see Daddy do that a lot, huh? You have one of those on your Lego tractor. [4 sec silence, then S pulls string on toy twice] It is difficult to say when the orchestra was invented because instruments have played together for many centuries. If we say that an orchestra is a group of string instruments with several players playing the same part, and that there may be wind instruments (i.e. woodwind and brass) or percussion playing as well, then the 17th century is the time that orchestras started. In Paris in 1626 King Louis XIII had an orchestra of 24 violins (called "24 Violons du Roi"). Later in the century the English king Charles II wanted to be like the French king and so he, too, had a string orchestra. Gradually the other instruments were added. At this time there was usually someone playing the harpsichord (the continuo part). It was often the composer himself, who would have conducted from the keyboard at important moments like the beginning and end of the piece. Green and yellow paint Strip of green card Glue (or stapler) Paint the middle of your plate and the lower half of the rim green. Paint the top half of the rim in yellow or your preferred other colour. Leave to dry. Now, using our photos as a guide, cut into the yellow rim to form the spikes. Again, do not cut into the centre of the plate. Save the scraps of yellow! Cut a tail from the green card, and stick to your plate. Use the yellow scraps that you cut off the plate to add spikes to the tail. Draw on a face. Contents / Part 3 / Previous chapter / Next chapter / Index You are probably already aware that as well as storing computer programs on cassette or disc, you can store "data". By "data" we mean sets of numbers or words. We might, for example, store a set of names and telephone numbers. This set of data is called a file. A set of BASIC statements are provided to enable you to read information from files, to write information to a new file, to update an existing file, to delete a file, to find out how big a file is, to move to a certain record in a file, to check if you have reached the end of a file and to obtain a catalogue of all the files that exist. There are also several other statements for performing other actions on the files. One of the major features of the BBC Computer system is that exactly the same statements are used no matter which 'filing system' is in use. A number of different filing systems are available including cassette, disc and network systems. Programs written to work an a cassette file system will usually work unmodified on a disc system. See page 400 for details of other file systems. Firstly here is a summary of all the file handling statements available in BBC BASIC. *CAT gives a catalogue of all data files and programs on the cassette or floppy disc. It takes a very long time on a cassette. OPENIN Opens a file so that it can be read. OPENOUT Opens a new (empty) file for writing. INPUT# Reads data from a file into the computer. PRINT# Writes data from the computer into a file. BGET# Reads a single character (byte) from a file. BPUT# Writes a single character (byte) to a file. PTR# Can be used either to find out which record we are about to read (or write) or to move to a specific record. (Not cassette) EXT# Indicates how long a file is. (Not cassette) EOF# Indicates whether or not the end of a file has been reached CLOSE# Indicates to the computer that you have finished with a file. The statement *CAT can be used at any time. However before you can use any of the other file statements you have to OPEN the file. After you have opened a file you can read and write to it as much as you wish. When you have finished with the file you must close it. An analogy may help to make one or two points clearer. The files are all kept in one room and your only method of communicating with them is via 5 telephone lines to 5 clerks. In addition there is a supervisor who knows which telephone line to use to communicate with the right clerk. It is the clerk's job to keep all the files tidy and you really have no idea how he looks after the files - nor does it matter, so long as he is efficient. To return to our computer, suppose that we wish to create a file called "DRINKS" in which we list every drink we ever try. First of all we have to ask the supervisor to allocate a phone line and clerk to us. The statement. will place the number of the channel (telephone line) allocated to the file into the variable X. Next we can ask the user for the names of the drinks using INPUT "WHAT IS THE DRINK CALLED", D$ and then send the name (held in D$) to the clerk to be entered in the file. Notice how we have to use the variable X to ensure that it is entered in the correct file. and then we could repeat this process until the user replied with the word STOP. The program would look like this 10 X=OPENOUT "DRINKS" 30 INPUT"What is the drink called ", D$ 40 PRINT#X, D$ 50 UNTIL D$="STOP" When run the program will save the data on cassette (if one is connected). RECORD then RETURN What is the drink called?WHISKY What is the drink caLLed?PORT What is the drink called?GIN What is the drink caLLed?BEER What is the drink caLLed!LAGER What is the drink called?CIDER What is the drink caLLed?TOMATO JUICE What is the drink called?STOP That has created a file called "DRINKS" which has been stored on cassette or disc. The program to read the file back in is also quite straightforward. 10 Y=OPENIN "DRINKS" 30 INPUT#Y , R$ 40 PRINT R$ 50 UNTIL R$="STOP" 60 CLOSE# Y When this is run, if the tape is now played from a point just before the position where the data was recorded, the list will then appear on the screen. When using these programs on cassette you must be careful to position the tape (by "forward" and "rewind") so that the tape is in the right place. In the first program, where you record the data onto the tape you will have to find a piece of blank tape - preferably with the tape recorder counter set to 100 or 200 or 300 or some other round number. After you have finished the first program you have to rewind the tape to the start of the data file which was at, say, 200. It is just like trying to play a piece of music back that you have recorded on a cassette; you must rewind the cassette to the start of the piece. You may well know how difficult it is to find a particular record on a cassette and that is why it is so important to use the tape counter every time and to write down where each title is on the tape. Your index might look like For most applications that is all you will need to know about file handling and you will only use statements like these "FILENAME" is the name of the file which normally consists of up to ten letters but see page 397 for more details. A and B$ represent any (and as many) numeric and string variables as you wish to record. X is a numeric variable used to remember the channel number allocated to the file number. For more specialist applications a number of other functions and statements are provided. BGET# and BPUT# enable single characters to be input and output. They would be used for recording special data, for example from laboratory experiments. EXT# and PTR# are mainly used with disc systems where random access files are required. They cannot be used in a cassette environment. EOF# enables a program to detect the end of the file when reading in data. It is normally used in the following way 10 Y=OPENIN "DRINKS" 30 INPUT#Y, A$ 40 PRINT A$ 50 UNTIL EOF#Y One of the programs on the WELCOME cassette can be used to keep a personal telephone directory. Clearly it should be possible to save a copy of all your entries on to cassette and to load them back into the computer later. Several modifications must be made to the program to enable this to happen. These modifications are shown below. Once you have modified the program you can then save the corrected version with a new name, for example First, load the program. Don't RUN it, but type CTRL N then LIST to list the program in 'page mode'. To move down the program, press SHIFT. When you come to a page requiring one of the changes set out below, press ESCAPE and edit the line in the normal way. Lines 210 to 280 omit final ' Add new lines: 282 PRINT" 9 - Load data from cassette" 284 PRINT" 0 - Save data to cassette" Change line 290 to 290 SEL = -1 In line 300 change TAB(3,22) to TAB(3,23) Line 330 change to 330 IF A<0 OR A>9 THEN 310 Change line 350 to 350 IF SEL> -1 THEN PRINT TAB(2,SEL+3 -10*(SEL=0));CHR$(&89); Change line 360 to 360 PRINT TAB(2,A+3-10*(A=0));CHR$(&88) Change line 380 to 380 IF SEL=-1 THEN 300 Change line 500 to 500 ON SEL +1 GOTO 505, 510,520,530,540 550,560,570,580,590 505 PROCSAVE:GOTO 200 590 PROCLOAD:GOTO 200 10000 DEF PROCLOAD 10005 PRINT TAB(0,16); "Play tape and press any key" 10008 PRINT "Please wait" 10010 E%=OPENIN "DATA" 10030 FOR 1%=1 TO X 10034 PROCPACK (I%) 11000 DEF PROCSAVE 11005 PRINT TAB(0,16); "Please press "; 11010 E%=OPENOUT "DATA" 11015 PRINT '"Please wait" 11030 FOR I%=1 TO X [places a fourth nut on top of the three already there] That house next door is Arte's house. This contributes significantly to your attractiveness. * Tips & Guidelines: + Consume a variety of fruits, vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins, and healthy fats. The bullet struck Booth in the back of the head behind his left ear, passed though his neck, and out into the barn. A low scream of pain like that produced by a sudden throttling came from the assassin, and he pitched headlong to the floor. Corbett and the other soliders would note a sense of poetic, or cosmic, justice in that Lincoln and Booth were each shot around the same spot of the head. And the damage to Booth was no less severe than that to Lincoln: the bullet had pierced three vertebrae and partially severed his spinal cord, paralyzing him. Their conditions were different as well, as Mary Clemmer Ames summed it up, "The balls entered the skull of each at nearly the same spot, but the trilling difference made an immeasurable difference in the sufferings of the two. Mr. Lincoln was unconscious of all pain, while his assassin suffered as exquisite agony as if he had been broken on a wheel." A soldier poured water into his mouth, which he immediately spat out, unable to swallow. The bullet wound prevented him from swallowing any of the liquid. In a weak voice, Booth asked for water and Conger and Baker gave it to him. He asked them to roll him over and turn him facedown. Conger thought it a bad idea. Then at least turn me on my side, the assassin pleaded. They did, but Conger saw that the move did not relieve Booth's suffering. Baker noticed it, too: "He seemed to suffer extreme pain whenever he was moved...and would several times repeat, 'Kill me.'" At sunrise, Booth remained in agonising pain. His pulse weakening as his breathing became more laboured and irregular. In agony, unable to move his limbs, he asked a soldier to lift his hands before his face and whispered as he gazed at them, "Useless ... Useless." These were his last words. A few minutes later, Booth began gasping for air as his throat continued to swell, then there was a shiver and a gurgle and his body shuddered, before Booth died from asphyxia - he literally choked to death. In 1956 the second was defined in terms of the period of revolution of the Earth around the Sun for a particular epoch, because by then it had become recognized that the Earth's rotation on its own axis was not sufficiently uniform as a standard of time. The Earth's motion was described in Newcomb's Tables of the Sun, which provides a formula for the motion of the Sun at the epoch 1900 based on astronomical observations made between 1750 and 1892. The second thus defined is You don't say you can't have it. Hello young scholars! Today, let's talk about something fun and useful - functions and their inverses. You may wonder, "What are those?" Well, imagine you have a magic box that changes every number you put into it. This magic box or "function" takes your number, performs some secret operation, and gives you a new number. An "inverse" function undoes whatever the magic box did so you can get back your original number. Cool, huh?! Now, there's a rule for creating our special magic boxes. A function should follow the 'One Input, One Output' rule. That means if you give it a 3, it cannot spit out both a 6 and a 10; it must give either a 6 or a 10, but consistently. Functions that follow this rule are called 'one-to-one.' So, why can't all functions have an inverse? Imagine having two different magic boxes doing opposite things. When you put in a 2, sometimes one box returns a 4 while the other returns a 6 - which output should the inverse choose? It can't decide because these conflicting boxes broke our 'One Input, One Output' rule. Thus, a function doesn't have an inverse if it's not a one-to-one function. To check if our magic box follows the rule, draw some lines horizontally across its picture (the 'graph'). If any line touches the graph at more than one spot, then the function isn't one-to-one and won't have an easy-peasy inverse. But if no line crosses the graph twice, congratulations! Your function obeys the rule, and it deserves a fantastic inverse! Let's practice finding the inverses of some friendly grade-school functions together soon! Keep exploring, keep learning! Calculating the Rate of Increase The rate of increase is a fundamental concept in mathematics that describes how quickly a quantity changes over time. This could be used to model population growth, the spread of disease, or financial investments. Understanding how to calculate this value can help you make informed decisions about real-world situations. In this tutorial, we will walk through the steps required to calculate the rate of increase using a mathematical formula. We'll also provide some helpful tips and guidelines along the way. Step 1: Identify the Initial Value (A) and Final Value (B) To begin with, you need to identify the initial value (A) and final value (B). The initial value represents the starting point for your data set, while the final value indicates where the data ends up after some period of time has passed. For example, if you are studying the growth of a bacterial culture, the initial value might represent the number of bacteria at time zero, while the final value would reflect the total number of bacteria present at some later point in time. Key Tip: Make sure you clearly label both values as either A or B so that there is no confusion when applying the formula. Step 2: Determine the Time Interval ( t) Next, determine the length of time between the initial value and the final value. This interval is denoted by t, which stands for "change in time." It may be given explicitly, such as "5 days" or "3 hours," or you may have to infer it from context. Regardless, knowing the exact duration of time elapsed is crucial because it directly impacts the calculated rate of increase. Guideline: Be precise with units; ensure they match throughout all calculations. Step 3: Apply the Formula Now that you have identified the necessary components, you can apply the following formula to calculate the rate of increase (r): r = (B - A) / ( t * n) Here, 'n' refers to the number of times the change occurs during the specified time interval. If the change happens only once, then n=1. However, if the change occurs multiple times within the same time frame (e.g., exponential growth), you must account for those additional instances by setting n equal to the appropriate number. Let's break down this formula further: * The numerator (B - A) calculates the difference between the final value and the initial value. This gives us an idea of how much our quantity has grown or shrunk during the observed period. * The denominator includes two factors: first, the time interval itself ( t); second, the frequency of change (n). Dividing the difference in values by these combined elements allows us to derive a per-unit rate of change. Example calculation: Suppose a population starts at 100 individuals and grows to 140 over the course of 7 years. To find the rate of increase, plug the values into the formula like this: r = (140 - 100) / (7 \* 1) = 50 / 7 7.14 So, the rate of increase for this population is approximately 7.14 individuals per year. Key Tips: * Double-check your arithmetic to avoid errors. * Keep track of units consistently throughout the problem. By following these three simple steps and keeping the provided guidelines in mind, you should now feel confident in your ability to calculate the rate of increase in various scenarios. Whether analyzing biological populations, economic trends, or other dynamic systems, understanding rates of change provides valuable insights into their underlying behavior. When the father had exerted his authority, and used other means in order to reconcile them, and all to no purpose, he at last had recourse to this expedient: he ordered his Sons to be called before him, and a short bundle of sticks to be brought; and then commanded them, one by one, to try if, with all their might and strength, they could any of them break it. They all tried, but to no purpose; for the sticks being closely and compactly bound up together, it was impossible for the force of man to do it. After this the father ordered the bundle to be untied, and gave a single stick to each of his Sons, at the same time bidding him try to break it, which, when each did, with all imaginable ease, the father addressed himself to them to this effect: "O, my sons, behold the power of unity! for if you, in like manner, would but keep yourselves strictly joined in the bonds of friendship, it would not be in the power of any mortal to hurt you; but when once the ties of brotherly affection are dissolved, how soon do you fall to pieces, and become liable to be violated by every injurious hand that assaults you." MORAL. Generating a number series in Microsoft Excel can be a useful skill for various purposes such as data analysis, modeling, or creating charts. This tutorial will guide you through the process step-by-step, providing detailed explanations along the way. ### Step 1: Open Microsoft Excel Start by launching Microsoft Excel on your computer. You can do this by searching for "Excel" in the Start menu (Windows) or Spotlight search (Mac), then clicking on the Excel icon. ### Step 2: Create a New Workbook Upon opening Excel, create a new workbook by selecting "New" from the startup screen or pressing `Ctrl + N` (or `Cmd + N` on a Mac). A blank worksheet will appear, ready for input. ### Step 3: Determine the Type of Series Decide what kind of number series you want to generate. Common types include arithmetic sequences (where each term increases or decreases by a constant difference), geometric sequences (where each term is multiplied or divided by a constant factor), and pattern-based sequences. The following steps will cover generating an arithmetic sequence; adjust accordingly if you need another type of series. ### Step 4: Identify the First Term and Common Difference Identify the first term of your series (the initial value) and the common difference (the amount added or subtracted between consecutive terms). For example, if you want to create the series {2, 5, 8, 11, ...}, the first term is 2, and the common difference is 3. ### Step 5: Enter the First Term into a Cell Enter the first term of your series into any cell within the worksheet. Click on the cell where you'd like the first term to reside, then simply type in the value and press `Enter`. For our running example, click on cell A1 and enter '2'. ### Step 6: Select the Cell with the First Term Click on the cell containing the first term of your series to select it. This action prepares us to fill subsequent cells with additional terms based on the chosen sequence rules. ### Step 7: Use AutoFill Feature to Generate the Sequence Use Excel's AutoFill feature to quickly populate adjacent cells with values that adhere to your specified sequence. To access AutoFill, hover over the bottom right corner of the selected cell until a small square appears (known as the Fill Handle). Then drag this handle downward across multiple rows while holding down the left mouse button. As you release the mouse button, the series should automatically expand throughout those cells. ### Step 8: Confirm Proper Generation of the Series Check whether the generated sequence matches the expected pattern. If necessary, correct errors manually or repeat Steps 6-7 after verifying proper inputs for the first term and common difference. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Remember to identify both the first term and the common difference before starting. These two pieces of information are essential for correctly generating the intended sequence. * Utilize the Fill Handle efficiently - it saves time when adding large sets of sequential numbers. * Double-check the resulting series against expectations. Human error might lead to incorrect entries initially, but catching these mistakes early ensures accurate results overall. oh, is this a happy pumpkin or a sad pumpkin? Fake an Ankle Sprain: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction Sometimes, there may be situations where feigning an injury can help you get out of uncomfortable or challenging circumstances. While we don't encourage dishonesty, this guide is designed to provide detailed information on faking an ankle sprain for those who genuinely need it, such as actors preparing for a role or individuals participating in pranks or harmless jokes. Please remember that using this knowledge irresponsibly or maliciously could lead to serious consequences. Step 1: Understand the Basics of an Ankle Sprain An ankle sprain occurs when the ligaments surrounding the ankle joint are stretched beyond their limits or tear due to sudden twisting, rolling, or awkward movement. Symptoms include pain, swelling, bruising, stiffness, and difficulty walking. To effectively fake an ankle sprain, you must understand these symptoms and mimic them accurately.
